content:
Someone reminded me of this old advice of mine:

"To keep learning things, it's crucial to keep asking lots of
questions, which involves making a fool of oneself. I often
notice that the most famous mathematicians and physicists are
the ones who ask the most questions, including a fair number
of dumb questions, in seminars - while meanwhile the grad students,
completely lost, are busy trying to look like they know what's going
on. Of course, if they work it correctly, people with a reputation
for being smart can get into the position of being allowed to
ask lots of questions: everyone knows they're smart, they don't
need to prove it, so they can act dumb and get away with it."
-- John Baez, sci.physics 8 Dec 1997.
So Socrates strategy was very smart then: He always claimed he knew nothing, and asked a lot of questions. :-)
He died at a wise age, and the memory of his questions shaped 2500 years of thought.

A big debt is owed to his student Plato, who wrote up all those dialogues. And he owed a debt to the Pythagoreans who saved him from being sold as a slave by the King of Sicily, where he perhaps asked some unwise questions.

I was just kidding: death by hemlock. Socrates comes off great in Plato's dialogues, which I love, but I. F. Stone has an interestingly different view:

  • I. F. Stone, I. F. Stone breaks the Socrates story, New York Times, April 8, 1979.
If "someone" happens to have been me asking questions which I later realised to be silly in one of the threads from the past few days, then I am honoured!

I completely back your advice @John Baez. Once you get into the habit of asking questions without worrying whether they sound silly, perhaps the next skill to learn is when to ask such questions on behalf of others (e.g. recognising linguistic or notational shortcuts that people with a more advanced understanding tend to make and elucidating them for others). You also manage to set the example in that regard :wink:
